signals.title: USD/JPY — Geopolitical Shock + Carry + Options
signals.text: USD/JPY at 158.91 — US-Iran diplomacy headlines regarding the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz in 30 days have triggered a 7% collapse in WTI oil to $96.6, pressuring the USD. Despite the risk-on relief, the +278bp OIS carry advantage (SOFR 3.51% vs TONA 0.73%) remains the structural floor. Options markets are aggressively hedging the downside with a -1.41 moderate put skew (25d RR), while COT shows a crowded JPY net short of -81,624. The combination of geopolitical de-escalation and crowded shorts suggests a floor near 158.00 unless VIX (16.6) spikes above 25.

signals.title: NZD/USD — COT vs OIS Divergence + RBNZ
signals.text: NZD/USD COT LF net short -15,435 (specs fading hawkishness) vs RBNZ hike bias priced for the 27 May meeting. With NZD real rates at -0.83% (most accommodative in G8) and the pair testing 0.5875, a hawkish RBNZ delivery poses a significant short-squeeze risk. Speculative positioning is misaligned with the OIS forward bias; any signal of a terminal rate hike targets a move toward 0.6000. Watch for the RBNZ rate decision on 27 May to trigger the squeeze.

signals.title: AUD/USD — Volatility Premium + Positioning + Retail
signals.text: AUD/USD at 0.7178 — IV at 13.8% vs HV30 at 8.9% generates a +4.9% Vol Risk Premium, the second highest in the G8, signaling extreme demand for downside protection. This aligns with a -0.85 moderate put skew in 25d RR. However, a massive institutional vs retail divergence is active: COT LF net long +57,180 (74.5% long) vs retail 76% short. Institutional conviction remains bullish despite the high cost of hedging; retail capitulation on a break of 0.7200 determines the next leg higher.

signals.title: EUR/USD — Volatility Premium + Carry
signals.text: EUR/USD at 1.1647 — IV at 7.1% vs HV30 at 4.9% creates a +2.2% VRP, indicating the options market is pricing in more realized volatility than the recent range suggests. The +175bp nominal carry advantage (Fed 3.75% vs ECB 2.0%) continues to favor USD, supported by a superior US Economic Surprise Index (CESI) net of +24. Upside remains structurally limited by the carry deficit and restrictive US real rates (+1.09%) vs accommodative EUR real rates (-0.32%).
